raw_response: {"rating": -3.5, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is an opinion/editorial piece (organizational position statement) from an environmental advocacy group. The author's own prose consistently advocates for expansive federal policy intervention, frames corporate and industry opposition as disinformation, and emphasizes social justice framing — all ideological markers of the left. The strength and consistency of these patterns across the full piece warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author explicitly calls for 'significant federal policy that puts a price on carbon' and 'large-scale investment in research and development,' framing expansive government intervention as the necessary solution — a distinctly left-leaning policy preference; (2) The author characterizes industry-aligned opponents in their own prose as spreading 'disinformation,' 'downplay and distort,' 'lobby for policies that reward polluters,' and 'undercut existing pollution standards' — consistently casting corporate and fossil fuel interests as bad-faith actors; (3) The author endorses 'a fast and fair phaseout of fossil fuels around the world' as an organizational position, a maximalist policy stance aligned with the left end of the climate policy spectrum; (4) The author frames climate investment priorities in social justice terms — 'socially just' and 'focused where the impacts are greatest—often in low-income communities and communities of color' — explicitly invoking equity-based redistribution language characteristic of left ideological framing; (5) The Paris Agreement is cited approvingly as 'the world's best effort to solve climate change so far,' endorsing international multilateral cooperation as a value, a preference associated with left-leaning foreign policy views; (6) No market-based, deregulatory, or individual-responsibility framings appear anywhere in the author's prose, leaving the piece entirely one-directional."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an opinion/editorial piece (organizational position statement) from an environmental advocacy group. The author's own prose consistently advocates for expansive federal policy intervention, frames corporate and industry opposition as disinformation, and emphasizes social justice framing — all ideological markers of the left. The strength and consistency of these patterns across the full piece warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author explicitly calls for 'significant federal policy that puts a price on carbon' and 'large-scale investment in research and development,' framing expansive government intervention as the necessary solution — a distinctly left-leaning policy preference; (2) The author characterizes industry-aligned opponents in their own prose as spreading 'disinformation,' 'downplay and distort,' 'lobby for policies that reward polluters,' and 'undercut existing pollution standards' — consistently casting corporate and fossil fuel interests as bad-faith actors; (3) The author endorses 'a fast and fair phaseout of fossil fuels around the world' as an organizational position, a maximalist policy stance aligned with the left end of the climate policy spectrum; (4) The author frames climate investment priorities in social justice terms — 'socially just' and 'focused where the impacts are greatest—often in low-income communities and communities of color' — explicitly invoking equity-based redistribution language characteristic of left ideological framing; (5) The Paris Agreement is cited approvingly as 'the world's best effort to solve climate change so far,' endorsing international multilateral cooperation as a value, a preference associated with left-leaning foreign policy views; (6) No market-based, deregulatory, or individual-responsibility framings appear anywhere in the author's prose, leaving the piece entirely one-directional.

explanation: This is an opinion/editorial piece (organizational position statement) from an environmental advocacy group. The author's own prose consistently advocates for expansive federal policy intervention, frames corporate and industry opposition as disinformation, and emphasizes social justice framing — all ideological markers of the left. The strength and consistency of these patterns across the full piece warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author explicitly calls for 'significant federal policy that puts a price on carbon' and 'large-scale investment in research and development,' framing expansive government intervention as the necessary solution — a distinctly left-leaning policy preference; (2) The author characterizes industry-aligned opponents in their own prose as spreading 'disinformation,' 'downplay and distort,' 'lobby for policies that reward polluters,' and 'undercut existing pollution standards' — consistently casting corporate and fossil fuel interests as bad-faith actors; (3) The author endorses 'a fast and fair phaseout of fossil fuels around the world' as an organizational position, a maximalist policy stance aligned with the left end of the climate policy spectrum; (4) The author frames climate investment priorities in social justice terms — 'socially just' and 'focused where the impacts are greatest—often in low-income communities and communities of color' — explicitly invoking equity-based redistribution language characteristic of left ideological framing; (5) The Paris Agreement is cited approvingly as 'the world's best effort to solve climate change so far,' endorsing international multilateral cooperation as a value, a preference associated with left-leaning foreign policy views; (6) No market-based, deregulatory, or individual-responsibility framings appear anywhere in the author's prose, leaving the piece entirely one-directional.
